title: Consequences for Social Security Fraud Act
short_title: Consequences for Social Security Fraud Act
plain_summary: This bill makes new rules about who can come to the United States and who has to leave. If someone who is not a U.S. citizen commits Social Security fraud, fake ID fraud, or fraud involving COVID-19 money programs, they cannot enter the country. If they are already in the United States, they can be forced to leave. The bill adds these crimes to the list of reasons why non-citizens can be denied entry or removed from America.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Consequences for Social Security Fraud Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Inadmissibility and Deportability Related to Social Security Fraud or Identification Document Fraud
sections.text: This section changes immigration laws to make certain people unable to enter or stay in the United States. People who are not US citizens cannot be admitted into the country if they have been convicted of certain crimes, admit they committed these crimes, or admit they did things that make up the basic parts of these crimes. The crimes include Social Security fraud, which means illegally using Social Security numbers or Social Security cards. It also includes identification document fraud, which means creating fake identification papers or using someone else's identification illegally. The section also covers COVID fraud, which means lying to get loans or grants that were created to help people during the COVID nineteen pandemic. These loans include those from the Small Business Act and grants from the American Rescue Plan Act and the Economic Aid to Hard Hit Small Businesses Act. People who planned with others to commit any of these crimes are also covered. Additionally, people who are already in the United States but are not citizens can be deported if they are convicted of these same crimes, admit to committing them, or admit to doing the basic acts that make up these crimes.
